sentences.chinese: 孝武皇帝好忠諫，說至言，〈師古曰：「說讀曰悅。」
sentences.literal: Emperor Wu loved loyal remonstrance and delighted in utmost words—〈Yan's note: "Yue is read yue 'delight.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wu welcomed blunt counsel and prized straight speech—Yan Shigu reads yue as delight.

sentences.chinese: 〉出爵不待廉茂，慶賜不須顯功，〈師古曰：「謂諫爭合意即得官爵，不由薦舉及軍功也。
sentences.literal: 〉he issued noble rank without waiting for "incorrupt" or "abundant talent," granted rewards without requiring manifest merit—〈Yan's note: "It means if remonstrance pleased him he gave office and rank without recommendation or military merit.
sentences.idiomatic: He handed out fiefs when a memorial pleased him—no need for the usual incorrupt or abundant-talent nominations or battle laurels.

sentences.chinese: 廉，廉吏也。
sentences.literal: Lian means an incorrupt official.
sentences.idiomatic: Lian names the "incorrupt" recommendation class.

sentences.chinese: 茂，茂材也。」
sentences.literal: Mao means abundant talent."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Mao names the "abundant talent" class."

sentences.chinese: 〉是以天下布衣各厲志竭精以赴闕廷自衒鬻者不可勝數。
sentences.literal: 〉Therefore every commoner in the realm sharpened his will and spent his wit to hurry to the palace gate to offer himself—beyond counting.
sentences.idiomatic: Commoners sharpened wits and thronged the capital to sell their service—uncountable.

sentences.chinese: 漢家得賢，於此爲盛。
sentences.literal: In this the Han house gained worthy men at its fullest tide.
sentences.idiomatic: Han talent peaked in that age.

sentences.chinese: 使孝武皇帝聽用其計，升平可致。
sentences.literal: Had Emperor Wu listened to their plans, the age of general peace could have been reached.
sentences.idiomatic: Had Wu used their advice, the realm might have reached true peace.

sentences.chinese: 〈張晏曰：「民有三年之儲曰升平。」
sentences.literal: 〈Zhang Yan's note: "When the people hold three years' stores it is called shengping."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Yan defines shengping as years when granaries hold three years of grain.

sentences.chinese: 〉於是積尸暴骨，快心胡越，故淮南王安緣間而起。
sentences.literal: 〉Then corpses were piled and bones exposed while hearts rejoiced over the Hu and Yue—therefore the king of Huainan, An, seized the gap and rose.
sentences.idiomatic: Instead came slaughter abroad and pride at crushing barbarians—so Liu An of Huainan saw his opening.

sentences.chinese: 所以計慮不成而謀議泄者，以衆賢聚於本朝，〈師古曰：「本朝，漢朝也。」
sentences.literal: The reason his plots never matured and his counsels leaked was that many worthies crowded this court—〈Yan's note: "Ben chao means the Han court."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Why the plot failed: talent still packed the Han capital—Yan Shigu glosses ben chao as our dynasty.

sentences.chinese: 〉故其大臣埶陵不敢和從也。
sentences.literal: 〉therefore his great ministers' power was checked and they dared not join in assent.
sentences.idiomatic: Huainan's ministers could not bully the ruler into conspiracy.

sentences.chinese: 〈服虔曰：「臣勢陵君也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Fu Qian's note: "The ministers' power overawed the ruler."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Fu Qian reads it as ministers dominating their lord.

sentences.chinese: 師古曰：「謂淮南大臣相內史之屬也。」
sentences.literal: Yan's note: "It means Huainan's ministers such as the chancellor and palace secretary."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Huainan's inner circle of high officials.

sentences.chinese: 〉方今布衣迺窺國家之隙，見間而起者，蜀郡是也。
sentences.literal: 〉Today commoners spy out cracks in the state; those who rise when they see a gap—Shu commandery is the case.
sentences.idiomatic: Today rustics watch for weakness—Shu rose first.

sentences.chinese: 〈孟康曰：「成帝鴻嘉中廣漢男子鄭躬等反是也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Meng Kang's note: "It means the Guanghan men Zheng Gong and others who rebelled in the Hongjia era of Cheng."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Meng Kang cites Zheng Gong's Guanghan rising under Cheng's Hongjia.

sentences.chinese: 〉及山陽亡徒蘇令之羣，蹈藉名都大郡，求黨與，索隨和，〈李竒曰：「求索與己和及隨己者。」
sentences.literal: 〉And the band of Shanyang fugitive Su Ling, trampling famous capitals and great commanderies, seeking allies, hunting for those who would follow or join them—〈Li Qi's note: "They sought men who would join them or go along with them."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Then Su Ling's Shanyang band overran great cities, recruiting allies—Li Qi glosses sui he as followers.

sentences.chinese: 〉而亡逃匿之意。
sentences.literal: 〉yet they showed no thought of fleeing into hiding.
sentences.idiomatic: They marched openly, never slinking into hiding.

sentences.chinese: 此皆輕量大臣，亡所畏忌，國家之權輕，故匹夫欲與上爭衡也。
sentences.literal: This all shows they hold the great ministers light, fear nothing, and the state's authority weighs little—so a commoner would match strength with the throne.
sentences.idiomatic: They despise high ministers because the court's awe has thinned—commoners dare face the Son of Heaven.

sentences.chinese: 士者，國之重器；
sentences.literal: Knights are the state's heavy vessels;
sentences.idiomatic: Talent is the dynasty's heavy armor;

sentences.chinese: 得士則重，失士則輕。
sentences.literal: gain knights and you are heavy, lose them and you are light.
sentences.idiomatic: with them you stand firm, without them you totter.

sentences.chinese: 詩云：「濟濟多士，文王以寧。」
sentences.literal: The Odes says, "In hosts so many the knights were—King Wen thereby had peace."
sentences.idiomatic: The Classic says many good men made King Wen secure.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「大雅文王之詩也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It is a poem in the Greater Ya on King Wen.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: from the Greater Ya "Wen Wang" ode.

sentences.chinese: 已解於上。」
sentences.literal: Already glossed above."〉
sentences.idiomatic: As explained earlier."

sentences.chinese: 〉廟堂之議，非草茅所當言也。
sentences.literal: 〉Deliberations of the temple hall are not what thatch cottages should speak of.
sentences.idiomatic: Inner-court policy is no topic for grass huts.

sentences.chinese: 臣誠恐身塗野草，尸并卒伍，故數上書求見，輒報罷。
sentences.literal: Your servant truly fears his body smeared on the wild grass, his corpse ranked among the ranks—therefore he has repeatedly memorialized asking audience, and each time is answered with dismissal.
sentences.idiomatic: I risk the execution ground yet keep petitioning—and each time you brush me off.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞齊桓之時有以九九見者，桓公不逆，欲以致大也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that in Duke Huan of Qi's time someone came with the multiplication table; Huan did not reject him, wishing thereby to draw in the great.
sentences.idiomatic: Duke Huan once heard a man teach the times table and still received him—hunger for greatness.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「九九，筭術，若今九章、五曹之輩。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Jiu jiu is reckoning, like today's Nine Chapters or Five Offices manuals."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: jiu jiu is arithmetic, like later mathematical primers.

sentences.chinese: 〉今臣所言非特九九也，陛下距臣者三矣，此天下士所以不至也。
sentences.literal: 〉What your servant speaks is more than the multiplication table—yet thrice Your Majesty has shut me out; that is why knights of the realm do not come.
sentences.idiomatic: My words outweigh a times table—three rebuffs prove why talent stays home.

sentences.chinese: 昔秦武王好力，任鄙叩關自鬻；
sentences.literal: Formerly King Wu of Qin loved strength; Ren Bi knocked at the gate to sell himself;
sentences.idiomatic: King Wu of Qin loved brawn; Ren Bi pounded the gate to offer his back;

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「秦武王即孝公之孫，惠文王之子也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "King Wu of Qin is the grandson of Duke Xiao, son of King Huiwen."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies the Qin king.

sentences.chinese: 任鄙，力士也。」
sentences.literal: Ren Bi was a strongman."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Ren Bi was a noted strongman."

sentences.chinese: 〉繆公行伯，繇余歸德。
sentences.literal: 〉Duke Mu acted as hegemon; You Yu came over out of virtue.
sentences.idiomatic: Duke Mu sought hegemony; You Yu defected out of respect.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「即秦穆公。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "That is Duke Mu of Qin."
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Qin Mugong.

sentences.chinese: 伯讀曰霸。
sentences.literal: Bo is read ba.
sentences.idiomatic: Bo here reads ba, hegemon.

sentences.chinese: 繇讀曰由。」
sentences.literal: You is read you."〉
sentences.idiomatic: You is read you."

sentences.chinese: 〉今欲致天下之士，民有上書求見者，輒使詣尚書問其所言，言可采取者，秩以升斗之祿，賜以一束之帛。
sentences.literal: 〉Now if you wish to draw knights of the realm, let anyone who submits a memorial asking audience be sent to the Imperial Secretary to be asked what he says; if the words may be adopted, give him rank with a few piculs of salary and grant a bundle of silk.
sentences.idiomatic: To summon talent, route every memorial to the Imperial Secretary; pay useful words with a token stipend and a bolt of silk.

sentences.chinese: 若此，則天下之士發憤懣，〈師古曰：「懣音滿。」
sentences.literal: If so, then knights of the realm will vent loyal resentment—〈Yan's note: "Men is read man."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Then honest rage will pour forth—Yan Shigu gives fanqie for men.

sentences.chinese: 〉吐忠言，嘉謀日聞於上，天下條貫，國家表裏，爛然可睹矣。
sentences.literal: 〉pour out loyal words, and good plans daily reach the ruler; the realm's threads, the state's inner and outer, will blaze clear to sight.
sentences.idiomatic: Good counsel will flow upward until every fiber of the realm lies plain.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「爛然，分明之貌也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ran ran means the appearance of clarity."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: ran ran means blazingly clear.

sentences.chinese: 〉夫以四海之廣，士民之數，能言之類至衆多也。
sentences.literal: 〉Within the four seas' breadth and the people's numbers, the articulate sort are exceedingly many.
sentences.idiomatic: Eloquent men swarm the four seas.

sentences.chinese: 然其儁桀指世陳政，言成文章，質之先聖而不繆，〈師古曰：「質，正也。」
sentences.literal: Yet those who point at the age and lay out policy, whose words form patterned essays, checked against the former sages without error—〈Yan's note: "Zhi means to correct."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yet few who write like classics and pass the sages' test—Yan Shigu: zhi is "test against."

sentences.chinese: 〉施之當世合時務，若此者，亦亡幾人。
sentences.literal: 〉applied to the present fit the times—such men are also not many.
sentences.idiomatic: Fewer still fit the present moment.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「無幾，言不多也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wu ji means not many.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: wu ji means scarce.

sentences.chinese: 幾音居豈反。」
sentences.literal: Ji is read ju-qi fan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Fanqie for ji."

sentences.chinese: 〉故爵祿束帛者，天下之厎石，〈師古曰：「厎，細石也，音之履反，又音秖。」
sentences.literal: 〉Therefore rank, stipend, and bundles of silk are the realm's whetstone—〈Yan's note: "Di is fine stone; read zhi-li fan, also read zhi."〉
sentences.idiomatic: So stipend and silk are the empire's grindstone—Yan Shigu glosses di as fine whetstone.

sentences.chinese: 〉高祖所以厲世摩鈍也。
sentences.literal: 〉this is how Gaozu sharpened the age and honed the blunt.
sentences.idiomatic: That is how Gaozu whetted the world's edge.

sentences.chinese: 孔子曰：「工欲善其事，必先利其器。」
sentences.literal: Confucius said, "The craftsman who wishes to do his work well must first sharpen his tools."
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said sharpen your tools before the job.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「論語載孔子之言也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The Analects records Confucius's words.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu cites the Analects.

sentences.chinese: 工以喻國政，利器喻賢材。」
sentences.literal: The craftsman stands for state affairs; sharp tools stand for worthy talent."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The craftsman is government; the tools are ministers."

sentences.chinese: 〉至秦則不然，張誹謗之罔，以爲漢𢿛除，倒持泰阿，授楚其柄。
sentences.literal: 〉Qin did otherwise: it spread a net of slander laws, thinking to clear the way for Han, yet held the Tai'e sword by the blade and handed Chu the hilt.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin stretched slander statutes to pave Han's rise—then gripped the Tai'e blade by the edge and gave the hilt to rebels.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「泰阿，劔名，歐冶所鑄也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Tai'e is a sword name, forged by Ou Yezi."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Tai'e was Ou Yezi's master blade.

sentences.chinese: 言秦無道，令陳涉、項羽乘間而發，譬倒持劔而以把授與人也。」
sentences.literal: It means Qin was lawless, letting Chen She and Xiang Yu seize the moment—like holding a sword upside down and offering the grip to another."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Lawless Qin let Chen She and Xiang Yu strike—an empire handing enemies the sword grip.

sentences.chinese: 〉故誠能勿失其柄，天下雖有不順，莫敢觸其鋒，此孝武皇帝所以辟地建功爲漢世宗也。
sentences.literal: 〉Therefore if you truly do not lose the hilt, though there be unruly men under Heaven none dare touch the edge—this is how Emperor Wu opened lands and built merit to become Han's Exalted Ancestor.
sentences.idiomatic: Keep the hilt and no rebel tests the edge—that is how Wu became the dynasty's martial ancestor.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「辟讀曰闢。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Pi is read pi 'to open.'"〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads pi as open or expand.

sentences.chinese: 〉今不循伯者之道，〈師古曰：「伯讀曰霸。
sentences.literal: 〉Now, not following the hegemon's path—〈Yan's note: "Bo is read ba.
sentences.idiomatic: Today, ignoring the hegemon's way—Yan Shigu: bo reads ba.

sentences.chinese: 次下亦同。」
sentences.literal: What follows below is the same."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The same reading recurs below."

sentences.chinese: 〉欲以三代選舉之法取當時之士，猶察伯樂之圖，求騏驥於市，而不可得，亦已明矣。
sentences.literal: 〉yet wishing to take the Three Dynasties' election methods to recruit men of the present is like studying Bo Le's horse chart to seek a swift steed in the market—unobtainable; it is already clear.
sentences.idiomatic: Using ancient selection rites to hire modern men is like finding a thoroughbred from a picture—hopeless and obvious.

sentences.chinese: 故高祖棄陳平之過而獲其謀，〈師古曰：「盜嫂受金之事。」
sentences.literal: Therefore Gaozu overlooked Chen Ping's faults and gained his stratagems—〈Yan's note: "The affair of stealing his sister-in-law and accepting gold."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Gaozu pardoned Chen Ping's scandals and took his plots—Yan Shigu cites the old gossip.

sentences.chinese: 〉晉文召天王，齊桓用其讎，〈師古曰：「召天王，謂狩于河陽也。
sentences.literal: 〉King Wen of Jin summoned the Son of Heaven; Duke Huan of Qi employed his enemy—〈Yan's note: "Summoning the Son of Heaven means the hunt at Heyang."
sentences.idiomatic: Jin Wen met the king at hunt; Huan used a foe as minister—Yan Shigu: the Heyang assembly.

sentences.chinese: 用其讎，謂以管仲爲相。
sentences.literal: Using his enemy means making Guan Zhong chancellor.
sentences.idiomatic: The enemy was Guan Zhong, made chief minister.

sentences.chinese: 並解於上。」
sentences.literal: Both glossed above."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Both stories glossed earlier."

sentences.chinese: 〉亡益於時，不顧逆順，此所謂伯道者也。
sentences.literal: 〉It brings no benefit to the times, ignores what is contrary or compliant—this is what is called the hegemon's way.
sentences.idiomatic: It ignores expedience yet works—that is hegemon politics.

sentences.chinese: 一色成體謂之醇，白黑雜合謂之駮。
sentences.literal: One color forming a whole is called chun; white and black mixed is called bo.
sentences.idiomatic: Pure hue is chun; mottled white and black is bo.

sentences.chinese: 欲以承平之法治暴秦之緒，〈師古曰：「緒謂餘業也。」
sentences.literal: To wish to govern the violent leavings of tyrant Qin with the statutes of full peace—〈Yan's note: "Xu means the remaining work."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Applying Wen-era statutes to Qin's wreckage—Yan Shigu: xu is leftover evil.

sentences.chinese: 〉猶以鄉飲酒之禮理軍市也。
sentences.literal: 〉is like ordering a village drinking rite to settle an army market.
sentences.idiomatic: is like running a village banquet in a war camp.

sentences.chinese: 今陛下旣不納天下之言，又加戮焉。
sentences.literal: Now Your Majesty neither accepts the words of the realm nor adds execution on top.
sentences.idiomatic: You reject counsel and pile executions on top.

sentences.chinese: 夫䳒鵲遭害，則仁鳥增逝；
sentences.literal: When owls and kites suffer harm, benevolent birds fly farther;
sentences.idiomatic: Harm hawks and phoenixes flee;

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「䳒，鴟也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "You is the owl.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: you names the owl.

sentences.chinese: 仁鳥，鸞鳳也。
sentences.literal: Benevolent birds are luan and feng.
sentences.idiomatic: Benevolent birds are luan and phoenix.

sentences.chinese: 䳒音緣。」
sentences.literal: You is read yuan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: You is read yuan."

sentences.chinese: 〉愚者蒙戮，則知士深退。
sentences.literal: 〉when fools are struck down, wise men withdraw deep.
sentences.idiomatic: Kill fools and the wise dig in.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「蒙，被也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Meng means to suffer."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: meng means suffer.

sentences.chinese: 〉間者愚民上疏，多觸不急之法，或下廷尉，而死者衆。
sentences.literal: 〉Recently commoners have submitted memorials that often brush the "not urgent" statutes; some are sent down to the Commandant of Justice—and many die.
sentences.idiomatic: Lately common memorials trip petty statutes; the Commandant of Justice swallows them and men die.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「以其所言爲不急而罪之也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Because their words were judged not urgent they were punished."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: punished for "non-urgent" speech.

sentences.chinese: 〉自陽朔以來，天下以言爲諱，朝廷尤甚，〈師古曰：「防人之口，法禁嚴切。」
sentences.literal: 〉Since Yangshuo the realm has taken speech as taboo, worst at court—〈Yan's note: "They plugged men's mouths; the statutes bit deep."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Since Yangshuo China fears its own tongue—worst in the hall—Yan Shigu: muzzling law.

sentences.chinese: 〉羣臣皆承順上指，莫有執正。
sentences.literal: 〉the host of ministers all follow the ruler's finger; none holds to the straight.
sentences.idiomatic: Every minister nods at your hint; none stands straight.

sentences.chinese: 何以明其然也？
sentences.literal: How may one know it is so?
sentences.idiomatic: How do we know?

sentences.chinese: 取民所上書，陛下之所善，試下之廷尉，廷尉必曰「非所宜言，大不敬。」
sentences.literal: Take what the people memorialize that Your Majesty praises, try sending it down to the Commandant of Justice—the Commandant will surely say, "Improper speech—great irreverence."
sentences.idiomatic: Take a memorial you praised and give it to the judge—he will call it lese-majeste.

sentences.chinese: 以此卜之，一矣。
sentences.literal: Judge by this—one proof.
sentences.idiomatic: That is one proof.

sentences.chinese: 故京兆尹王章資質忠直，敢面引廷爭，孝元皇帝擢之，以厲具臣而矯曲朝。
sentences.literal: Therefore Wang Zhang, former Governor of the Capital, by nature loyal and straight, dared face the court and argue in the open; Emperor Yuan raised him to warn placeholder ministers and straighten the bent court.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Zhang of the capital spoke truth to power; Yuan promoted him to shame seat-warmers.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「具臣，具位之臣無益者也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ju chen means ministers who hold place without benefit.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: ju chen warms a useless chair.

sentences.chinese: 矯，正也。」
sentences.literal: Jiao means to correct."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Jiao means set right."

sentences.chinese: 〉及至陛下，戮及妻子。
sentences.literal: 〉Under Your Majesty execution reached wife and children.
sentences.idiomatic: Under you his wife and children died too.

sentences.chinese: 且惡惡止其身，王章非有反畔之辜，而殃及家。
sentences.literal: Moreover to hate evil stops at the person's body—Wang Zhang had no crime of rebellion, yet calamity reached his household.
sentences.idiomatic: Punish the man, not the clan—Wang Zhang was no traitor, yet his house fell.

sentences.chinese: 折直士之節，結諫臣之舌，羣臣皆知其非，然不敢爭，天下以言爲戒，最國家之大患也。
sentences.literal: You have broken straight ministers' integrity and tied remonstrants' tongues; the host of ministers all know it is wrong yet dare not contend—the realm takes speech as warning: that is the state's greatest peril.
sentences.idiomatic: You broke honest spines and sealed loyal mouths; all know it is wrong yet stay mute—speech taboo is the worst peril.

sentences.chinese: 願陛下循高祖之軌，杜亡秦之路，〈師古曰：「杜，塞也。」
sentences.literal: I beg Your Majesty follow Gaozu's track and block Qin's road to ruin—〈Yan's note: "Du means to stop up."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Return to Gaozu's road and dam Qin's ditch—Yan Shigu: du is block.

sentences.chinese: 〉數御十月之歌，〈孟康曰：「福譏切王氏。
sentences.literal: 〉often take up the song of the tenth month—〈Meng Kang's note: "Fu satirizes the Wang clan.
sentences.idiomatic: Sing often the "Tenth Month" ode—Meng Kang says Fu attacks the Wangs.

sentences.chinese: 十月之詩，刺后族太盛也。」
sentences.literal: The tenth month's poem satirizes the queen clan growing too strong."〉
sentences.idiomatic: It warns when maternal kin swell."

sentences.chinese: 師古曰：「詩小雅十月之交篇也。」
sentences.literal: Yan's note: "It is the 'Shi jiao' piece in the Lesser Ya."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: from Lesser Ya "Shi yue zhi jiao."

sentences.chinese: 〉留意亡逸之戒，〈師古曰：「周書篇名也，周公作之以戒成王。」
sentences.literal: 〉heed the warning of "No Yi"—〈Yan's note: "A chapter title in the Zhou Documents, composed by the Duke of Zhou to warn King Cheng."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Study the "Wu yi" chapter—Yan Shigu: Zhou document to King Cheng.

sentences.chinese: 〉除不急之法，下亡諱之詔，博覽兼聽，謀及疏賤，令深者不隱，遠者不塞，所謂「辟四門，明四目」也。
sentences.literal: 〉abolish statutes that are not urgent, issue an edict forbidding no speech, read widely and listen broadly, take counsel even from the distant and base, so the deep are not hidden and the far are not blocked—what is called "open the four gates, brighten the four eyes."
sentences.idiomatic: Scrap petty speech laws, invite plain words, hear low and distant voices—open four gates, brighten four eyes.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「虞書舜典曰『闢四門，明四目』，言開四門以致衆賢，則明視於四方也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The Shun dian in the Documents says 'open the four gates, brighten the four eyes'—meaning open four gates to draw many worthies and see clearly in four directions."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu cites Shun's canon on opening the court to sight.

sentences.chinese: 〉且不急之法，誹謗之微者也。
sentences.literal: 〉Moreover statutes that are not urgent are the fine meshes of the slander laws.
sentences.idiomatic: Petty statutes are slander-law snares.

sentences.chinese: 「往者不可及，來者猶可追。」
sentences.literal: "What is past cannot be caught up; what comes may still be pursued."
sentences.idiomatic: The past is gone; the future can be mended.

sentences.chinese: 方今君命犯而主威奪，〈師古曰：「君命犯者，謂大臣犯君之命。」
sentences.literal: Now lordly commands are violated and sovereign awe is wrested away—〈Yan's note: "Lordly commands violated means great ministers defying the ruler's orders."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Ministers defy edicts and imperial awe fades—Yan Shigu explains.

sentences.chinese: 〉外戚之權日以益隆，陛下不見其形，願察其景。
sentences.literal: 〉maternal kin's power grows daily greater; Your Majesty does not see the form—please observe the shadow.
sentences.idiomatic: Consort power swells by the day—read the shadow if not the shape.

sentences.chinese: 建始以來，日食地震，以率言之，三倍春秋，水災亡與比數。
sentences.literal: Since Jianshi, by rough reckoning, solar eclipses and earthquakes have come three times the Spring and Autumn Annals' rate; floods cannot be matched in number.
sentences.idiomatic: Since Jianshi, eclipses and quakes triple the Annals' count; floods beyond reckoning.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「言其極多，不可比較而數也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It means they are extremely many—not to be compared or counted."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: too many to tally.

sentences.chinese: 〉陰盛陽微，金鐵爲飛，此何景也！
sentences.literal: 〉Yin waxes and yang wanes; metal and iron fly aloft—what shadow is this!
sentences.idiomatic: Yin swells, yang thins—metal flies from forges: what omen is that!

sentences.chinese: 〈張晏曰：「河平二年，沛郡鐵官鑄鐵如星飛上去，權臣用事之異也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Zhang Yan's note: "In the second year of Heping, molten iron at the Pei foundry flew like stars—an anomaly of mighty ministers at work."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Yan cites molten iron flying at Pei in Heping 2 as a sign of overmighty subjects.
